enclosureTypes.name: Dome Closure
keyFeatures: Cylindrical or dome-shaped body with removable end cap
Gel or mechanical (compression) cable port sealing
Multiple fiber entry ports (8–24 ports typical)
Re-enterable without special tooling in most models
Stackable splice trays — easy to add capacity within the same closure
Accepts both loose-tube and tight-buffer cable types
enclosureTypes.fieldNotes: The most common OSP splice closure type. Suited for all installation environments. Gel-sealed versions are preferred for direct buried and high-water-table locations. Mechanical-seal versions are cleaner for re-entry in vault and aerial work. Before ordering, size to cable count plus 20% spare tray capacity for future splices — undersized closures are a common field problem.

nerdStuff.title: Gel vs. Mechanical Sealing
nerdStuff.explanation: Gel-sealed domes use a petroleum-based gel that fills all air gaps and cable entry points. Gel provides excellent moisture protection over decades but complicates re-entry (messy, cold-weather gel hardens) and may require a gel-cleaning kit. Mechanical-seal domes use compression fittings and O-rings — clean re-entry and proper torque on all ports is required for a valid seal. In high-water-table and direct-buried applications, gel sealing is preferred. For aerial and vault installations where maintenance re-entry is expected, mechanical sealing is more practical.

enclosureTypes.name: Inline / Horizontal Closure
keyFeatures: Pass-through cable entry — the cable enters one end and exits the other without being cut
Clamshell or split-body design for quick tray access on a pole or strand
Strand mounting brackets or bail attachments for aerial lashing
Preserves cable strength member continuity through the closure
Horizontal splice tray orientation in most models
enclosureTypes.fieldNotes: Preferred for mid-span aerial applications where the cable continues through the closure. Only the needed fibers are broken out and spliced — the rest pass through untouched. Easier to open on a pole than a dome. Strand installation requires proper lashing hardware and load ratings — verify closure weight (full with trays and cable) against aerial span specifications.

enclosureTypes.name: Wall-Mount / Building Entry Terminal
keyFeatures: Fixed wall or backboard mounting
Indoor and weatherproof outdoor variants available
Often includes both splice tray slots and adapter panel (connector) positions
Accepts distribution cable on feeder side, drop cables on output side
MDU variants support FTTH drop cable slack storage
enclosureTypes.fieldNotes: Common at building entry points and MDU distribution heads. The indoor version provides organized cable management and port labeling. Outdoor weatherproof versions are IP-rated for exterior wall mounting. In MDU applications, locate the terminal in a lockable space — untrained residents will pull on fiber if it's accessible.

enclosureTypes.name: Rack-Mount Splice / Patch Enclosure
keyFeatures: 19-inch or 23-inch EIA rack compatible
Front-access trays in most designs — no need to pull the unit from the rack
Integrated cable routing with bend radius guides
Often combined with LC/SC/MPO adapter panel on the front face
High-density MPO/MTP modules available (72 or 144 fibers per 1U)
enclosureTypes.fieldNotes: Standard in central offices, equipment rooms, and data centers. The highest-density fiber termination option. Label every port before any fiber is terminated — in a 7-foot rack with 2000+ fibers, unlabeled ports will create permanent maintenance problems. Use patch cord management spools at every row.

enclosureTypes.name: Fiber Distribution Hub (FDH)
keyFeatures: Integrated PON splitter tray slots (1:4 to 1:64 PLC splitters)
Feeder cable input on one side, distribution and drop cable outputs on the other
Weatherproof housing rated for direct buried, aerial, or vault installation
Integrated patch panel for drop cable connections
Lockable — security-critical since the FDH serves multiple customers
enclosureTypes.fieldNotes: The FTTH/GPON distribution point where feeder fiber is split to customer drops. Size the splitter count conservatively — it is expensive to retrofit additional split capacity later. Document every port assignment (which port goes to which address) at time of installation. PON splitter insertion loss (≈17 dB for 1:64) is the largest single loss element in the GPON link budget.

nerdStuff.title: Optical Splitter Insertion Loss

formulas.name: Splitter Loss (theoretical)

variables.meaning: Split ratio — number of output ports

formulas.example: 1:32 splitter → 10 × log10(32) = 15.05 dB theoretical. Typical measured value: 16–17 dB including excess loss.

nerdStuff.explanation: Optical PLC splitters divide power equally across N output ports. Theoretical loss is 10×log(N). Actual measured loss is 1–2 dB higher due to excess loss in the planar lightwave circuit substrate. A 1:64 splitter has approximately 18 dB insertion loss — this single component consumes the majority of the GPON power budget (typically 28–32 dB total). Cable, splice, and connector losses must all fit within the remaining 10–14 dB margin.

notableModels.notes: The most commonly encountered dome closure in North American OSP. Accepts 450-series splice trays. Available in gel-sealed and mechanical-seal versions. Extremely widely stocked by OSP contractors.
Smaller dome for access network taps and distribution splice points. 400A is the original; 400B is the updated variant. Common on older installed plant.
High-capacity central office distribution box. Modular tray system supports incremental population.

brands.notes: Corning products are the de facto standard in North American OSP. The 450-series ecosystem (closure, trays, hardware, splice sleeves) is the most commonly encountered system in field work. When in doubt about tray compatibility, verify the closure series before ordering replacement trays.

notableModels.notes: Very common dome closure specified by major US Tier 1 carriers. Mechanical seal design for clean re-entry. Compatible with standard AFL 12-splice trays.
Larger FIST series dome with gel seal. Preferred for direct buried and high-moisture vault environments. Requires gel-removal kit for re-entry.

brands.notes: AFL closures are specified by several major US carriers. The FIST series is well-regarded for field re-enterability and splice tray quality. AFL also manufactures fusion splicers (Fujikura-branded) — common to see AFL closures used alongside AFL splicers on the same carrier projects.

notableModels.notes: Standard CommScope dome for distribution and feeder OSP. Used by carriers deploying CommScope end-to-end infrastructure.
Inline aerial closure for mid-span splicing on lashed cable plant. Strand-mount hardware included.

brands.notes: CommScope is a major presence in FTTH, enterprise, and carrier OSP. Their product line spans closures, FDHs, and rack systems, making them common on projects where the customer wants a single-vendor infrastructure solution.

notableModels.notes: Widely used by Tier 1 and Tier 2 carriers across all installation environments. Known for robust sealing and straightforward field re-entry. One of the most common non-Corning domes encountered in North American OSP.

brands.notes: PLP is a significant North American OSP presence, particularly for aerial and direct-buried applications. Their hardware (strand clamps, cable lashers, anchors) is ubiquitous alongside fiber closures.

spliceTrays.name: 12-Splice Standard Tray
spliceTrays.notes: The universal OSP splice tray. Holds 12 fusion or mechanical splices in individual heat-shrink sleeve holders. Two trays stacked = 24 positions. Used in virtually all OSP dome and inline closures. The 450-series standard is the most widely compatible.

spliceTrays.name: 24-Splice Standard Tray
spliceTrays.notes: Higher-capacity tray for large-count closures. Less common than 12-splice on distribution plant. Used in feeder and backbone closures with 144F+ cable counts where tray real estate is limited.

spliceTrays.name: 12-Ribbon Mass-Fusion Tray
spliceTrays.notes: Designed for mass-fusion ribbon splicing. Holds 12 ribbon splices × 12 fibers each = 144 fibers per tray. Used in high-count OSP (144F, 288F, 432F cables). Requires a ribbon-capable mass-fusion splicer — a single-fiber splicer cannot splice ribbons. Dramatically faster than individual fusion splicing at high fiber counts.

selectionGuide.scenario: Direct buried mid-span splice on distribution cable (24F–144F)
selectionGuide.notes: Dome with gel seal for maximum moisture protection. Size to cable fiber count plus 20% spare tray capacity. Mark closure location on as-built drawings — re-finding a buried closure without GPS coordinates is costly.

selectionGuide.scenario: Aerial mid-span splice on lashed ADSS or figure-8 cable
selectionGuide.notes: Inline horizontal for cable continuity through the closure. Strand-mount hardware required — verify closure weight (loaded) against aerial span design. Protect splice closure from traffic damage at pole level if near roadway.

selectionGuide.scenario: Underground vault splice on high-count feeder cable (288F+)
selectionGuide.notes: Large-capacity dome (FOSC-450 or equivalent) with ribbon tray capability for mass-fusion efficiency. Mechanical seal for clean vault re-entry. Leave adequate slack coil inside the vault for future splicing.

selectionGuide.scenario: FTTH distribution point / PON splitter location
selectionGuide.notes: FDH with integrated splitter tray slots sized for target split ratio (1:32 or 1:64). Include 25% spare port capacity for new customer connects. Weatherproof rating appropriate for installation environment. Lockable enclosure required — security is critical.

selectionGuide.scenario: Building entry terminal / MDU fiber head-end
selectionGuide.notes: Wall-mount terminal with splice and patch panel capability. Indoor weatherproof (IP-rated) version if in unconditioned space. Locate in a secured equipment room or locked closet when possible.

selectionGuide.scenario: Central office or equipment room termination
selectionGuide.notes: Rack-mount for high-density termination. Use MPO/MTP modules for 72F or 144F per 1U. Label every port at installation — retrofitting labels on a populated rack is a multi-hour job.
